title: Optimal State Preparation for Impulse Estimation in Gaussian Quantum Systems
authors: Kaspar Schmerling, Andreas Kugi, Andreas Deutschmann-Olek
abstract: We present an optimal control-based strategy to enhance the estimation of impulse-like disturbances in continuously monitored linear classical and quantum systems by exploiting non-equilibrium states. Using optimal estimation techniques for linear Gaussian systems to collect information from the temporal vicinity of the disturbance, we cast the minimization of disturbance estimation uncertainty as a nonlinear optimal control problem over time-dependent system parameters. The resulting method dynamically shapes the estimation covariances through parametric modulation, maximizing information gain at a known impulse time. This differs fundamentally from conventional squeezing protocols using periodic modulation that effectively degrade inference of impulse-like disturbances. Applied to nanomechanical resonators and levitated nanoparticles, optimal parametric driving reduces estimation variance by up to a factor of two relative to steady-state operation
summary: This paper presents an optimal control strategy to minimize the uncertainty in detecting sudden, impulse-like disturbances in Gaussian quantum systems. By dynamically shaping the system's covariance through parametric modulation, the method outperforms traditional periodic squeezing, which can actually hinder detection. The approach is demonstrated to be highly effective for platforms like nanomechanical resonators and levitated nanoparticles.
main_problem: Enhancing the estimation of impulse-like disturbances in continuously monitored linear Gaussian quantum systems by optimizing time-dependent system parameters.
main_result: The proposed optimal control strategy reduces estimation uncertainty by up to a factor of two compared to steady-state operation, demonstrating that conventional periodic squeezing can actually degrade impulse estimation.
method: The authors formulate the minimization of estimation uncertainty as a nonlinear optimal control problem, solved using the IPOPT solver and CasADi framework via a multiple shooting method.
model_or_system: The study focuses on Gaussian open quantum systems described by a quadratic Hamiltonian and a Stochastic Master Equation, with specific applications to nanomechanical resonators (NEMS) and levitated nanoparticles.
key_observables: Estimation covariance matrix, impulse magnitude uncertainty, and momentum/position quadrature error covariances.
important_parameters: Measurement efficiency, measurement rates, damping rate, resonance frequency, and modulation depth constraints.
main_assumptions: The impulse is assumed to be instantaneous (Dirac-like) and occur at a known time, and the system dynamics are assumed to be linear/quadratic (Gaussian).
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates covariance ellipse shaping; Figure 2 compares momentum uncertainty in NEMS for optimized vs. rectangular modulation; Figure 3 shows momentum uncertainty evolution and control signals for levitated nanoparticles; Figure 4 details the evolution of forward and backward filtering uncertainties.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of impulse estimation, establishes the Gaussian framework using Kalman-Bucy filtering and retrodiction, presents the optimal control formulation, and validates the method through numerical simulations of NEMS and levitated nanoparticles.
why_interesting: It provides a novel approach to non-equilibrium state preparation for sensing, showing that standard squeezing protocols are suboptimal for transient disturbances, which is highly relevant for continuous measurement and quantum sensing.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1. Illustration of the impulse estimation improvement by optimizing the covariance dynamics. Top: unopti- mized case. Bottom: optimized case with minimized variance along the p direction, e.g., for the case of a momentum kick.

Fig. 2. (a) Momentum uncertainty of the NEMS resonator. Red line: square root of the sum of forward and back- ward covariances with optimized modulation signal. Blue line: square root of the sum of forward and backward covariances for a rectangular modulation signal. (b) Optimized modulation signal. (c) Rectan- gular modulation with 2Ω0 frequency.

Fig. 3. (a) Momentum uncertainty of a levitated nanopar- ticle. Red line: square root of the sum of forward and backward covariances with optimized modulation signal. Blue line: square root of the sum of forward and backward covariances for a rectangular modulation signal. (b) Optimized modulation signal. (c) Rectan- gular modulation with 2Ω0 frequency and adjusted switching times.
Fig. 4. (a) Forward and backward momentum impulse estimation uncertainty for optimized protocol. (b) Momentum impulse estimation uncertainty with the simple modulation protocol.
